ideas.The dualism of practical reason:
The "**dualism of practical reason**" is Henry Sidgwick's most famous and troubling conclusion — the unresolved conflict, at the heart of ethics, between the demands of **egoism** (rational self-interest) and those of **utilitarianism** (the impartial pursuit of the general good). Reached at the end of his monumental *The Methods of Ethics*, this sobering result is one of the most important and honest confrontations with a deep problem in moral philosophy.

Sidgwick held that reason presents us with certain self-evident principles, and he found that *two* of these principles, each apparently rational and compelling, point in opposite directions. On the one hand, the principle of **rational egoism** ("prudence") holds that it is rational for each individual to seek their own greatest happiness — that my own good is a reasonable and self-evidently important end for me to pursue. On the other hand, the principle of **rational benevolence** (utilitarianism) holds that, from the impartial "point of view of the universe," the good of any one individual is no more important than the good of any other, so that reason requires me to promote the **greatest good on the whole**, even at the sacrifice of my own.

The problem is that these two principles can **conflict**: cases arise where sacrificing my own happiness would maximize the general happiness, so that egoism and utilitarianism give contradictory verdicts about what I rationally *ought* to do. And Sidgwick could find no rational way to **reconcile** them or to establish the priority of one over the other. Both seem equally rational, yet they cannot both be fully satisfied. This left him with the "dualism of practical reason" — a fundamental, apparently irresolvable contradiction within our practical rationality itself. Sidgwick regarded this as a genuine failure to make ethics fully coherent, and his refusal to paper over it — his intellectual honesty in admitting the impasse — has made the dualism a lasting and much-discussed problem in ethics.
ideas.Methodical comparison of ethical theorie:
Henry Sidgwick's most distinctive methodological contribution — and the organizing principle of his masterwork *The Methods of Ethics* — was his rigorous, systematic, and impartial **comparison of the major methods of ethical reasoning**. Rather than simply defending one moral theory and attacking the others, Sidgwick undertook to examine, with unprecedented care and fairness, the different fundamental ways in which people actually reason about what they ought to do, analyzing each on its own terms and testing it against the others.

Sidgwick identified three principal "**methods of ethics**" — three distinct procedures for determining right conduct, each grounded in a different ultimate principle. **Egoism** (or rational self-interest) reasons that one ought to do whatever maximizes one's own happiness. **Intuitionism** holds that we grasp certain moral rules or duties as self-evidently right through direct moral intuition, independent of their consequences (this corresponds roughly to common-sense morality with its array of particular duties). And **utilitarianism** holds that one ought to do whatever maximizes the general happiness of all. Sidgwick's project was to investigate each of these methods thoroughly — clarifying its principles, working out its implications, and assessing its coherence and self-evidence.

What made his approach so influential was its **analytical rigor, impartiality, and honesty**. Sidgwick refused to force a premature resolution; he followed the arguments wherever they led, acknowledging the genuine strengths of each method and the difficulties of each. He argued, importantly, that intuitionism, when its vague common-sense rules are made precise and consistent, tends to point *toward* utilitarianism — so that the two can be partly reconciled. But egoism and utilitarianism, he concluded, could *not* be reconciled, leaving the famous "dualism of practical reason." This model of careful, systematic, even-handed comparative analysis set a new standard for rigor in moral philosophy and made Sidgwick a founding figure of modern analytic ethics.
ideas.Common-sense morality:
Henry Sidgwick devoted a central portion of his ethical inquiry to the careful examination of "**common-sense morality**" — the ordinary, everyday moral rules, duties, and intuitions that most people accept without question (such as the duties to keep promises, tell the truth, be just, show gratitude, and care for one's family). His subtle and rigorous critical analysis of this common moral consciousness is one of the most important parts of *The Methods of Ethics* and a landmark in the study of moral intuition.

Sidgwick took common-sense morality seriously as a candidate "method of ethics" — the **intuitionist** view that we grasp a plurality of particular moral duties as self-evidently binding, independent of any calculation of consequences. He examined these commonly accepted duties one by one, with great care, asking whether they really meet the standards required of genuine self-evident moral axioms: whether they are **clear and precise** in their meaning, **consistent** with one another, and truly **self-evident** and free from exceptions.

His conclusion was subtly critical. Sidgwick found that the maxims of common-sense morality, however useful in practice, **fail** to be truly precise, unconditional, and mutually consistent when subjected to rigorous scrutiny. Each common duty, examined closely, turns out to be vague, to admit of exceptions, and to require qualification — and the various duties can conflict with one another, with no clear principle to resolve the conflicts. Common-sense morality is therefore not a set of genuinely self-evident axioms but a body of rough, practically valuable but philosophically imprecise rules. Crucially, Sidgwick argued that when we try to make these rules **precise and consistent**, and seek a deeper principle to systematize and correct them, we are driven *toward* the **utilitarian** principle — so that utilitarianism can be seen as the rational completion and foundation of common-sense morality. This influential analysis showed both the value and the limits of moral intuition and became a model for the study of ethics.

works.The Methods of Ethics:
*The Methods of Ethics* (1874) is Henry Sidgwick's masterpiece and one of the most important and rigorous works of moral philosophy ever written — widely regarded as the culmination of the classical **utilitarian** tradition and a founding text of modern analytic ethics. The philosopher C. D. Broad called it "on the whole the best treatise on moral theory that has ever been written," and its careful, systematic method continues to influence ethical theory (it deeply shaped, for instance, Derek Parfit's work).

Rather than simply advocating a single moral doctrine, Sidgwick undertakes a rigorous, impartial **comparison of the principal "methods of ethics"** — the fundamental procedures by which people reason about what they ought to do. He identifies three: **egoism** (maximize one's own happiness), **intuitionism** (follow the self-evident duties of common-sense morality), and **utilitarianism** (maximize the general happiness). With unmatched analytical care and fairness, Sidgwick examines each method — clarifying its principles, drawing out its implications, and testing its coherence and self-evidence.

His central findings are subtle. He subjects **common-sense (intuitionist) morality** to searching scrutiny and finds its maxims imprecise and inconsistent when rigorously examined — but argues that, when refined and systematized, intuitionism points *toward* utilitarianism, so that the two can be substantially reconciled. He also holds that certain genuinely self-evident axioms (of justice, prudence, and rational benevolence) can be identified, providing a rational foundation for utilitarianism. But the work famously ends on a note of unresolved tension: the "**dualism of practical reason**," the apparently irreconcilable conflict between rational egoism and rational benevolence, which Sidgwick honestly confesses he cannot resolve. Combining systematic rigor, intellectual honesty, and profound insight, *The Methods of Ethics* set a new standard for moral philosophy and remains a touchstone of the field.
